docs.examples.cache_extension.FragmentCa.documentation:
## Summary: A Jinja2 extension that provides template fragment caching functionality through a custom "cache" tag. ## Description: This extension enables caching of template fragments by implementing a custom "cache" tag that can be used in Jinja2 templates. When a template contains a {%- cache key %}...{%- endcache %} block, the extension caches the rendered content of that block. On subsequent renders, if cached content exists for the given key, it returns the cached version instead of re-rendering the block. The extension integrates with Jinja2's template parsing and execution system by defining a custom tag and providing caching support through the environment's fragment_cache mechanism. ## State: - fragment_cache_prefix (str): Prefix prepended to cache keys, defaults to empty string - fragment_cache (object): Cache implementation used for storing cached content, defaults to None - tags (set): Defines the tag names this extension handles, specifically {"cache"} ## Lifecycle: - Creation: Instantiate with a Jinja2 Environment object - Usage: Template containing {%- cache key %}...{%- endcache %} blocks is parsed and rendered - Destruction: No explicit cleanup required; relies on Environment lifecycle management ## Method Map: ```mermaid graph TD A[Template Parse] --> B[parse()] B --> C[CallBlock with _cache_support] C --> D[_cache_support()] D --> E{Cache Hit?} E -->|Yes| F[Return Cached Value] E -->|No| G[Render Block] G --> H[Store in Cache] H --> F ``` ## Raises: - None explicitly raised by __init__ - May raise exceptions from underlying cache implementation during get/add operations ## Example: ```jinja2 {%- cache "user_profile_" + user.id %}

{{ user.name }}

{{ user.email }}

{%- endcache %} ``` This would cache the rendered HTML block under a key combining the prefix and the cache key expression, returning cached content if available.

## Summary:
Initializes an optimizer instance with an optional environment for expression evaluation.

## Description:
Sets up the optimizer with an optional environment that will be used for evaluating expressions during the optimization process. This method is part of the initialization lifecycle of the optimizer and prepares the instance for subsequent operations.

## Args:
    environment (Optional[Environment]): An optional environment object that provides context for expression evaluation. When None, the optimizer operates without environment-specific context.

## Returns:
    None: This method does not return any value.

## Raises:
    None: This method does not raise any exceptions.

## State Changes:
    Attributes READ: None
    Attributes WRITTEN: self.environment

## Constraints:
    Preconditions: None
    Postconditions: The optimizer instance will have its environment attribute set to the provided value or None.

## Side Effects:
    None: This method performs no I/O operations or external service calls.

src.jinja2.utils.Cycler.__init__.documentation:
## Summary:
Initializes a Cycler object with a sequence of items to cycle through, setting the initial position to zero.

## Description:
The Cycler.__init__ method constructs a cycling iterator that maintains a position index and allows sequential access to a collection of items in a circular manner. This method validates that at least one item is provided and initializes the internal state for cycling operations.

## Args:
    *items (t.Any): Variable-length argument list of items to cycle through. Must contain at least one item.

## Returns:
    None: This method does not return a value.

## Raises:
    RuntimeError: Raised when no items are provided to the constructor, ensuring the Cycler always has at least one item to cycle through.

## State Changes:
    Attributes READ: None
    Attributes WRITTEN: 
        - self.items: Set to the tuple of provided items
        - self.pos: Initialized to 0 (first position in the cycle)

## Constraints:
    Preconditions:
        - At least one item must be provided as an argument
    Postconditions:
        - self.items contains all provided items in order
        - self.pos is initialized to 0
        - The Cycler is ready for cycling operations

## Side Effects:
    None: This method performs no I/O operations or external service calls. It only initializes object attributes.

src.jinja2.utils.Namespace.__init__.documentation:
## Summary:
Initializes a Namespace instance with attribute key-value pairs stored in an internal dictionary.

## Description:
The `__init__` method sets up the internal `__attrs` dictionary that stores all attributes for the Namespace instance. It accepts a single iterable of key-value pairs (such as a dictionary or list of tuples) and/or keyword arguments, combining them into a single dictionary that becomes the internal storage for the namespace.

## Args:
    *args: Variable length argument list containing either a single iterable of key-value pairs or multiple key-value pairs as separate arguments.
    **kwargs: Arbitrary keyword arguments representing key-value pairs to store in the namespace.

## Returns:
    None: This method does not return any value.

## Raises:
    TypeError: If the arguments cannot be converted to a dictionary (e.g., invalid argument types).

## State Changes:
    Attributes READ: None
    Attributes WRITTEN: Sets `self.__attrs` to a dictionary containing all provided key-value pairs.

## Constraints:
    Preconditions: The arguments must be compatible with the `dict()` constructor.
    Postconditions: After execution, `self.__attrs` contains all key-value pairs from both positional and keyword arguments.

## Side Effects:
    None: This method performs no I/O operations or external service calls.

src.jinja2.sandbox.SandboxedEnvironment..documentation:
## Summary:
Initializes a SandboxedEnvironment instance with secure default configurations and restricted operations.

## Description:
This constructor method sets up a Jinja2 environment with enhanced security measures by initializing the global namespace with a safe range function and copying default binary/unary operation tables. It extends the base Environment class to provide a sandboxed execution context that prevents potentially dangerous operations.

## Args:
    *args (Any): Variable length argument list passed to the parent Environment.__init__ method
    **kwargs (Any): Arbitrary keyword arguments passed to the parent Environment.__init__ method

## Returns:
    None: This method initializes the object in-place and does not return a value

## Raises:
    None explicitly raised by this method, though parent class initialization may raise exceptions

## State Changes:
    Attributes READ: self.default_binop_table, self.default_unop_table
    Attributes WRITTEN: self.globals, self.binop_table, self.unop_table

## Constraints:
    Preconditions: The parent Environment class must accept the provided arguments
    Postconditions: The SandboxedEnvironment instance will have:
        - A globals dictionary containing a safe_range function
        - Copied binop_table and unop_table from default tables
        - The sandboxed flag set to True (inherited from class definition)

## Side Effects:
    None: This method performs in-place initialization and doesn't cause external I/O or mutations

src.jinja2.lexer.Failure.__init__.documentation:
## Summary:
Initializes a Failure object with an error message and exception class for later use in raising template syntax errors.

## Description:
The Failure class serves as a factory for creating exception-raising objects. This constructor stores the error message and the exception class type that will be used when the Failure instance is called. It's designed to be used in lexer error handling contexts where a delayed exception creation is needed.

## Args:
    message (str): The error message to be included in the raised exception.
    cls (Type[TemplateSyntaxError], optional): The exception class to raise. Defaults to TemplateSyntaxError.

## Returns:
    None: This method does not return a value.

## Raises:
    None: This method does not raise exceptions directly.

## State Changes:
    Attributes READ: None
    Attributes WRITTEN: 
        - self.message: Stores the error message string
        - self.error_class: Stores the exception class type

## Constraints:
    Preconditions: 
        - message must be a string
        - cls must be a subclass of TemplateSyntaxError or compatible exception class
    Postconditions: 
        - self.message contains the provided error message
        - self.error_class contains the provided exception class

## Side Effects:
    None: This method performs no I/O operations or external service calls.

src.jinja2.lexer.TokenStream.__init__.documentation:
## Summary:
Initializes a TokenStream object with a token generator and optional metadata, setting up the internal state for token iteration.

## Description:
The TokenStream constructor prepares the object for token iteration by initializing internal state variables, setting up the token generator, and priming the stream with an initial token. This method establishes the foundational state needed for subsequent token operations like iteration, pushing, and lookahead.

## Args:
    generator (Iterable[Token]): An iterable of Token objects that provides the token stream.
    name (Optional[str]): Name identifier for the template, used in error reporting.
    filename (Optional[str]): Filename of the template source, used in error reporting.

## Returns:
    None: This method initializes the object in-place and does not return a value.

## Raises:
    None explicitly raised: The method itself doesn't raise exceptions, though underlying iterator operations might.

## State Changes:
    Attributes READ: None
    Attributes WRITTEN: 
    - self._iter: Set to iter(generator) to create an iterator from the token generator
    - self._pushed: Initialized as an empty deque for storing pushed-back tokens
    - self.name: Set to the provided name parameter
    - self.filename: Set to the provided filename parameter
    - self.closed: Set to False to indicate the stream is initially open
    - self.current: Set to a Token with line number 1, type TOKEN_INITIAL, and empty string value

## Constraints:
    Preconditions:
    - The generator parameter must be iterable over Token objects
    - The Token class must be properly defined with lineno, type, and value attributes
    - TOKEN_INITIAL must be a valid token type constant
    
    Postconditions:
    - The TokenStream instance is ready for iteration
    - self.current is initialized to a valid Token object
    - The stream is marked as not closed

## Side Effects:
    None: This method performs no I/O operations or external service calls. It only initializes internal state and consumes the first token from the generator via the next() call.

src.jinja2.lexer.Lexer.__init__.documentation:
## Summary:
Initializes a Jinja2 template lexer with environment-specific tokenization rules and configuration settings.

## Description:
Configures the lexer with environment-dependent regex patterns and tokenization rules for parsing Jinja2 templates. This method sets up the core parsing infrastructure including regular expressions for identifying different token types (whitespace, numbers, names, strings, operators) and state-based parsing rules for handling template constructs like blocks, variables, comments, and raw sections.

## Args:
    environment (Environment): The Jinja2 environment containing template configuration such as block delimiters, variable delimiters, and formatting options.

## Returns:
    None: This method initializes instance attributes and does not return a value.

## Raises:
    None explicitly raised by this method.

## State Changes:
    Attributes READ: environment.block_start_string, environment.block_end_string, environment.comment_end_string, environment.variable_end_string, environment.trim_blocks, environment.lstrip_blocks, environment.newline_sequence, environment.keep_trailing_newline, environment.line_statement_prefix, environment.line_comment_prefix
    Attributes WRITTEN: self.lstrip_blocks, self.newline_sequence, self.keep_trailing_newline, self.rules

## Constraints:
    Preconditions: The environment parameter must be a valid Environment instance with properly configured delimiter strings and formatting options.
    Postconditions: The lexer instance will have initialized self.rules dictionary containing all necessary tokenization rules for template parsing.

## Side Effects:
    None: This method only initializes internal state and does not perform I/O operations or mutate external objects.

docs.examples.cache_extension.FragmentCa.documentation:
## Summary:
Implements a caching mechanism for Jinja2 template fragments by checking cache, generating content when needed, and storing results with timeout.

## Description:
This method provides a caching layer for template fragment rendering in Jinja2. When invoked, it first attempts to retrieve a cached value using a constructed key based on the fragment cache prefix and name. If the value exists in cache, it's returned immediately without executing the caller function. Otherwise, it executes the provided caller function to generate the template fragment content, stores it in cache with the specified timeout, and returns the generated value. This method is internally called by the Jinja2 template engine when processing cache blocks.

## Args:
    name (str): The cache key identifier for the template fragment
    timeout (int or None): Cache expiration time in seconds, or None for no expiration
    caller (callable): Function that generates the template fragment content when cache misses

## Returns:
    Any: The cached value if found, or the newly generated value from caller()

## Raises:
    None explicitly raised

## State Changes:
    Attributes READ: self.environment.fragment_cache_prefix, self.environment.fragment_cache
    Attributes WRITTEN: None directly modified, but self.environment.fragment_cache is modified via add() call

## Constraints:
    Preconditions: 
    - self.environment.fragment_cache must be a cache-like object with get() and add() methods
    - self.environment.fragment_cache_prefix must be a string
    - caller must be callable
    Postconditions:
    - If cache miss occurs, the generated value is stored in self.environment.fragment_cache
    - The returned value is either cached or newly generated

## Side Effects:
    - Reads from self.environment.fragment_cache
    - Writes to self.environment.fragment_cache via add() operation
    - Calls the provided caller() function which may have its own side effects

docs.examples.cache_extension.FragmentCa.documentation:
## Summary:
Parses the `{% cache %}` template tag syntax and constructs a CallBlock node for cached template fragment rendering.

## Description:
This method is part of the Jinja2 Extension framework and handles the parsing of the `{% cache %}` template tag. It processes the cache tag's arguments, parses the cached content block, and generates a CallBlock node that will invoke the `_cache_support` method during template rendering. This method is automatically invoked by Jinja2's template parser when it encounters a `{% cache %}` tag.

## Args:
    parser (Parser): The Jinja2 parser object that contains the template stream and parsing context.

## Returns:
    CallBlock: A Jinja2 CallBlock node representing the parsed cache directive that will execute cached content rendering.

## Raises:
    None explicitly raised, though underlying parser methods may raise exceptions during parsing.

## State Changes:
    Attributes READ: 
    - self.environment.fragment_cache_prefix (accessed via environment)
    - self.environment.fragment_cache (accessed via environment)
    
    Attributes WRITTEN: 
    - None directly modified, but indirectly affects cache state through _cache_support method calls

## Constraints:
    Preconditions:
    - The parser must be positioned at a valid `{% cache %}` tag token
    - The parser stream must contain valid expressions for the cache arguments
    - The template must contain a matching `{% endcache %}` closing tag
    
    Postconditions:
    - Returns a properly constructed CallBlock node with correct line number set
    - The returned node will execute the `_cache_support` method during template rendering

## Side Effects:
    None directly, but the resulting CallBlock node will cause:
    - Cache lookups in the environment's fragment_cache during template rendering
    - Potential cache additions if content is not found in cache
    - Execution of the cached template content block via the caller() function

docs.examples.inline_gettext_extension.I.documentation:
## Summary:
A Jinja2 extension that converts inline gettext expressions into proper Jinja2 translation blocks.

## Description:
This class extends Jinja2's Extension base class to provide a mechanism for converting inline gettext expressions (such as _("hello world")) into proper Jinja2 trans/endtrans blocks during template compilation. It processes the token stream to identify and transform these expressions, enabling easy internationalization of Jinja2 templates.

The extension specifically targets "data" tokens in the template stream, searching for patterns that match gettext expressions and converting them into appropriate Jinja2 syntax blocks.

## State:
- paren_stack: int - Tracks nesting level of parentheses in gettext expressions, initialized to 0 at the start of each token processing
- The class doesn't store persistent state beyond the processing of individual streams

## Lifecycle:
- Creation: Instantiated as part of Jinja2 environment configuration
- Usage: Called automatically by Jinja2 during template compilation when the extension is registered via the filter_stream method
- Destruction: Managed by Jinja2's lifecycle management

## Method Map:
```mermaid
graph TD
    A[filter_stream] --> B{token.type == "data"?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Process token value for gettext expressions]
    B -- No --> D[Yield token unchanged]
    C --> E[Search for gettext patterns using _outside_re/_inside_re]
    E --> F{Pattern match found?}
    F -- Yes --> G{Escape sequence?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Yield escaped character]
    G -- No --> I{Inside parentheses?}
    I -- No --> J[Start trans block]
    I -- Yes --> K[Handle parentheses nesting]
    F -- No --> L[Yield remaining unprocessed data]
    J --> M[Yield trans block tokens]
    K --> N[Yield data or endtrans tokens]
    L --> O[Continue processing]
```

## Raises:
- TemplateSyntaxError: When encountering unclosed gettext expressions in the template stream, indicating malformed inline gettext syntax

## Example:
```python
# Register extension with Jinja2 environment
from jinja2 import Environment
env = Environment(extensions=['docs.examples.inline_gettext_extension.InlineGettext'])

# Template with inline gettext expressions
template_source = '''Hello %(name)s! %( _("Welcome to our site") )s'''

# The extension converts _("Welcome to our site") into:
# {% trans %}Welcome to our site{% endtrans %}
```

It processes the token stream to identify and transform these expressions, enabling easy internationalization of Jinja2 templates.
The extension specifically targets "data" tokens in the template stream, searching for patterns that match gettext expressions and converting them into appropriate Jinja2 syntax blocks.

## Summary:

## Description:

It handles nested parentheses and properly manages the parsing state to ensure correct conversion of gettext syntax. The method is designed to work as part of a Jinja2 extension that enables inline gettext syntax in templates, converting expressions like `_(Hello World)` into `{% trans %}Hello World{% endtrans %}` blocks.

## Returns:

    Attributes WRITTEN: None

## Constraints:
    Preconditions: The input stream must be a valid Jinja2 token stream

## Side Effects:
    None

scripts.generate_identifier_pattern.buil.documentation:
## Summary:
Converts character ranges into a compact string representation for identifier pattern matching.

## Description:
Processes a sequence of character pairs representing ranges and formats them into a compact string. This function is designed to optimize character range representations by eliminating redundancy in common cases like single characters or consecutive characters.

## Args:
    ranges (iterable): An iterable of character pairs (a, b) where a and b are characters defining a range. Each pair represents a character range from a to b inclusive.

## Returns:
    str: A compact string representation where:
        - Single characters are represented as-is (e.g., 'a')
        - Consecutive characters are represented as separate characters (e.g., 'ab' for range 'a'-'b')
        - General ranges are represented in dash notation (e.g., 'a-z')

## Raises:
    None explicitly raised, but may raise exceptions from iteration over ranges or string operations if input is malformed.

## Constraints:
    Preconditions:
        - Input ranges must be iterable containing pairs of characters
        - Each pair must contain valid characters (string length 1)
        - Characters in each pair must be comparable via ord() function
    
    Postconditions:
        - Output string contains only printable ASCII characters or dashes
        - All input ranges are properly represented in the output

## Side Effects:
    None

## Control Flow:
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start build_pattern] --> B{Iterate ranges}
    B --> C{a == b?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Append a]
    C -->|No| E{ord(b) - ord(a) == 1?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Append a, Append b]
    E -->|No| G[Append "{a}-{b}"]
    D --> H[Next range]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{More ranges?}
    I -->|Yes| B
    I -->|No| J[Return joined string]
```

## Examples:
    >>> build_pattern([('a', 'z')])
    'a-z'
    >>> build_pattern([('a', 'a'), ('b', 'c')])
    'abc'
    >>> build_pattern([('a', 'b'), ('d', 'f')])
    'abdf'
    >>> build_pattern([('0', '9')])
    '0-9'
```

scripts.generate_identifier_pattern.coll.documentation:
## Summary:
Groups consecutive ASCII characters into maximal contiguous ranges by identifying sequences where each character's ASCII value equals the previous character's ASCII value plus one.

## Description:
This function efficiently identifies and collapses contiguous character sequences using a mathematical grouping technique. It leverages the property that for consecutive ASCII characters, the difference between `ord(character)` and `index` remains constant. By grouping elements with identical differences using `itertools.groupby`, it efficiently identifies maximal contiguous character ranges.

This utility is commonly used in identifier pattern generation where consecutive characters need to be represented as compact range specifications rather than individual characters.

## Args:
    data (iterable): An iterable of characters to be grouped into contiguous ranges

## Returns:
    generator: A generator yielding tuples of (start_char, end_char) representing the start and end of each contiguous character range in the input

## Raises:
    None explicitly raised

## Constraints:
    Preconditions:
    - Input data should be iterable containing characters
    - Characters should be comparable via ASCII values
    
    Postconditions:
    - Each yielded tuple contains two characters where the second character's ASCII value is greater than or equal to the first character's ASCII value
    - The returned ranges represent maximal contiguous sequences in the input
    - Empty input produces no output

## Side Effects:
    None

## Control Flow:
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input data] --> B[Enumerate data]
    B --> C[Group by ord(char) - index]
    C --> D{Has group?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Convert group to list]
    E --> F[Get first char from group]
    F --> G[Get last char from group]
    G --> H[Yield (first, last)]
    D -->|No| I[End]
```

## Examples:
    >>> # Collapse a simple range
    ... list(collapse_ranges('abc'))
    [('a', 'c')]
    
    >>> # Non-consecutive characters remain separate
    ... list(collapse_ranges('ace'))
    [('a', 'a'), ('c', 'c'), ('e', 'e')]
    
    >>> # Mixed consecutive and non-consecutive
    ... list(collapse_ranges('abcdfg'))
    [('a', 'c'), ('d', 'g')]
    
    >>> # Empty input
    ... list(collapse_ranges(''))
    []
    
    >>> # Single character
    ... list(collapse_ranges('x'))
    [('x', 'x')]
```

scripts.generate_identifier_pattern.get_.documentation:
## Summary:
Generates Unicode characters suitable for use in Python identifiers that are not standard word characters.

## Description:
This function systematically examines all Unicode code points to identify characters that can be used as part of Python identifiers but are not classified as word characters. It's designed to help discover special Unicode characters that extend Python's identifier naming capabilities beyond ASCII letters and digits.

The function isolates this logic because it performs a specific filtering operation on Unicode space to find characters meeting two criteria: (1) when prepended with 'a', the result forms a valid Python identifier, and (2) the character itself is not considered a word character by regex pattern matching.

## Args:
    None

## Returns:
    Generator[str]: A generator yielding Unicode characters that satisfy both conditions:
        1. ("a" + character).isidentifier() returns True
        2. not re.match(r"\w", character) returns True

## Raises:
    None explicitly raised

## Constraints:
    Preconditions:
    - sys.maxunicode must be defined and provide a valid upper bound for Unicode code points
    - The function processes all Unicode code points from 0 to sys.maxunicode, which is computationally intensive
    
    Postconditions:
    - Every yielded character satisfies both boolean conditions in the filter
    - The generator produces characters in ascending order of Unicode code point

## Side Effects:
    None

## Control Flow:
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B[Iterate code points 0 to sys.maxunicode]
    B --> C[Convert code point to character]
    C --> D[Check if "a" + character is identifier]
    D --> E{Is identifier?}
    E -->|No| F[Continue to next code point]
    E -->|Yes| G[Check if character is not word character]
    G --> H{Is not word character?}
    H -->|No| I[Continue to next code point]
    H -->|Yes| J[Yield character]
    J --> K[Next iteration]
    F --> K
    I --> K
    K --> L{More code points?}
    L -->|Yes| B
    L -->|No| M[End]
```

## Examples:
```python
# Basic usage to get first few special identifier characters
chars = get_characters()
first_five = [next(chars) for _ in range(5)]
# Returns characters like: ['_', 'À', 'Á', 'Â', 'Ã']

# Find all non-ASCII identifier-compatible characters
special_chars = []
for char in get_characters():
    if ord(char) > 127:  # Non-ASCII characters only
        special_chars.append(char)
        if len(special_chars) >= 10:  # Limit for demonstration
            break

# Usage in identifier pattern generation context
identifier_chars = list(get_characters())
print(f"Found {len(identifier_chars)} special identifier characters")
```

The function isolates this logic because it performs a specific filtering operation on Unicode space to find characters meeting two criteria: (1) when prepended with 'a', the result forms a valid Python identifier, and (2) the character itself is not considered a word character by regex pattern matching.

scripts.generate_identifier_pattern.main.documentation:
## Summary:
Generates a regex pattern for validating Unicode identifiers and writes it to a source file.

## Description:
This function serves as a code generation script that creates a regex pattern for identifying valid Unicode identifiers. It collects Unicode characters that are valid in identifiers but not part of standard word characters, groups them into ranges, builds a regex pattern, and writes the result to a generated Python file. This approach ensures proper Unicode identifier support in the Jinja2 templating engine.

## Args:
    None

## Returns:
    None

## Raises:
    None explicitly raised

## Constraints:
    Preconditions:
    - The script must be run from the scripts/ directory
    - The target directory structure (src/jinja2/) must exist
    - Python's sys.maxunicode must be available for character enumeration
    
    Postconditions:
    - A file named _identifier.py will be created in src/jinja2/
    - The file will contain a compiled regex pattern for identifier validation

## Side Effects:
    - Creates or overwrites a file at src/jinja2/_identifier.py
    - Writes to the filesystem

## Control Flow:
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start main()] --> B[Get Unicode characters]
    B --> C[Collapse into ranges]
    C --> D[Build regex pattern]
    D --> E[Determine output filename]
    E --> F[Write to file]
    F --> G[End]
```

## Examples:
This function is typically invoked as a standalone script to regenerate identifier patterns when Unicode support requirements change. It does not require explicit invocation in normal usage, as it's designed to be run manually when needed.

src.jinja2.utils.pass_eval_context.documentation:
## Summary:
Decorator that marks a function to receive the Jinja2 evaluation context as an argument.

## Description:
This decorator is used to indicate that a function should be called with the Jinja2 evaluation context as its first argument. It sets an internal attribute (`jinja_pass_arg`) on the decorated function to signal to the Jinja2 template engine that the evaluation context should be passed when invoking the function.

## Args:
    f (F): The function to be decorated, where F is a callable type that will receive the evaluation context.

## Returns:
    F: The same function with the `jinja_pass_arg` attribute set to `_PassArg.eval_context`.

## Raises:
    None explicitly raised by this function.

## Constraints:
    Preconditions:
    - The function `f` must be callable
    - The global `_PassArg` must be defined with an `eval_context` member
    
    Postconditions:
    - The returned function has the attribute `jinja_pass_arg` set to `_PassArg.eval_context`
    - The original function `f` is unchanged except for the added attribute

## Side Effects:
    None - This function only modifies the metadata of the input function by adding an attribute.

## Control Flow:
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[pass_eval_context called with function f] --> B{Function f exists?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Set f.jinja_pass_arg = _PassArg.eval_context]
    C --> D[Return function f]
    B -- No --> E[Raises TypeError from Python internals]
```

## Examples:
```python
@pass_eval_context
def my_template_function(eval_ctx, arg1, arg2):
    # This function will receive the evaluation context as first argument
    return eval_ctx.resolve(arg1) + arg2

# Usage in Jinja2 template:
# {{ my_template_function("variable_name", 42) }}
```

src.jinja2.async_utils.async_variant.documentation:
## Summary:
Creates a decorator that enables switching between synchronous and asynchronous function implementations based on execution context.

## Description:
The `async_variant` function is a decorator factory designed to support both synchronous and asynchronous versions of the same function within Jinja2 templates. It allows a single interface to work in both async and sync contexts by automatically selecting the appropriate implementation based on the environment's async capability.

This abstraction is particularly useful in template engines where the same operation might need to be performed either synchronously or asynchronously depending on whether the rendering context is async-capable. The decorator handles the complexity of determining which version to invoke based on runtime context.

## Args:
    normal_func (Callable): The synchronous version of the function to be wrapped. This function serves as the base implementation that will be called when the execution context is synchronous.

## Returns:
    Callable: A decorator function that accepts an asynchronous version of the function and returns a wrapper that intelligently routes calls to either the synchronous or asynchronous implementation.

## Raises:
    None explicitly raised by this function. However, the underlying functions being decorated may raise exceptions.

## Constraints:
    Preconditions:
    - The `normal_func` parameter must be a callable that can be properly wrapped
    - The async function passed to the returned decorator must be compatible with the normal function signature
    - The function objects must have proper metadata for the wrapping process to work correctly
    - The normal function should be designed to work in both sync and async contexts
    
    Postconditions:
    - The returned wrapper function maintains the signature and metadata of both the normal and async functions
    - The wrapper function will execute either the synchronous or asynchronous version based on the execution context
    - The wrapper function will have a `jinja_async_variant` attribute set to `True`
    - The wrapper function will properly handle argument passing based on the `_PassArg` configuration

## Side Effects:
    None directly caused by this function. However, the wrapper function it produces may cause side effects depending on the functions it wraps.

## Control Flow:
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[async_variant called with normal_func] --> B[Returns decorator]
    B --> C[decorator called with async_func]
    C --> D[Get pass_arg from normal_func using _PassArg.from_obj]
    D --> E[Set need_eval_context = (pass_arg is None)]
    E --> F[Define is_async function based on pass_arg]
    F --> G[Create wrapper function with wraps decorators]
    G --> H[In wrapper: call is_async(args) to determine context]
    H --> I{is_async returns True?}
    I -->|Yes| J[If need_eval_context: args = args[1:] then call async_func(*args, **kwargs)]
    I -->|No| K[If need_eval_context: args = args[1:] then call normal_func(*args, **kwargs)]
    J --> L[Return async result]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Return result]
```

## Examples:
```python
# Define a synchronous function that may be used in both sync and async contexts
@async_variant
def render_template(name, context):
    # Synchronous implementation
    return f"Rendered {name} with {context}"

# Create async variant using the decorator
@render_template.async_variant
async def render_template_async(name, context):
    # Asynchronous implementation  
    return f"Async rendered {name} with {context}"

# When called in sync context:
result = render_template("test.html", {"user": "john"})  # Calls sync version

# When called in async context:
result = await render_template("test.html", {"user": "john"})  # Calls async version

# The wrapper automatically detects the execution context and chooses appropriately
```

src.jinja2.async_utils.auto_await.documentation:
## Summary:
Asynchronously resolves awaitable values while preserving regular values, enabling seamless handling of mixed sync/async contexts.

## Description:
This utility function provides a unified interface for processing values that may be either synchronous values or awaitable objects. It automatically detects and awaits awaitable objects while returning non-awaitable values unchanged. This pattern is commonly used in template engines like Jinja2 where expressions might evaluate to either regular values or coroutines that need to be resolved.

The function is extracted into its own utility to provide a clean abstraction for handling mixed sync/async workflows without requiring callers to manually check for awaitability.

## Args:
    value (Union[Awaitable[V], V]): A value that may be either an awaitable object or a regular value of type V. The type V represents the expected return type.

## Returns:
    V: The resolved value, either the original value if it was not awaitable, or the result of awaiting the awaitable value. The return type matches the input type V.

## Raises:
    None explicitly raised by this function, though underlying await operations may raise exceptions during execution.

## Constraints:
    Preconditions:
    - The input value must be compatible with the type system expectations
    - If the value is an awaitable, it must be a valid awaitable object that can be awaited
    
    Postconditions:
    - The returned value will be of the same type as the input value (type V)
    - If the input was awaitable, the returned value will be the awaited result
    - If the input was not awaitable, the returned value will be identical to the input

## Side Effects:
    None - This function is pure and doesn't modify external state

## Control Flow:
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start auto_await] --> B{type(value) in _common_primitives?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Return value casted to V]
    B -- No --> D{inspect.isawaitable(value)?}
    D -- Yes --> E[await value and return result]
    D -- No --> F[Return value casted to V]
```

## Examples:
```python
# Usage with regular values
result = await auto_await(42)  # Returns 42

# Usage with awaitable values  
async def get_value():
    return "hello"
    
result = await auto_await(get_value())  # Returns "hello" after awaiting
```

src.jinja2.async_utils.auto_aiter.documentation:
## Summary:
Converts a synchronous or asynchronous iterable into an asynchronous iterator.

## Description:
This utility function provides a unified interface for iterating over both synchronous and asynchronous iterables. It automatically detects whether the input supports asynchronous iteration and handles it appropriately, making it easier to write code that works with both types of iterables without explicit type checking.

## Args:
    iterable (Union[AsyncIterable[V], Iterable[V]]): An iterable object that may be either synchronous or asynchronous. Must support either `__aiter__` method for async iteration or standard iteration protocol.

## Returns:
    AsyncIterator[V]: An asynchronous iterator that yields items from the input iterable, regardless of whether it's synchronous or asynchronous.

## Raises:
    None explicitly raised - however, underlying iteration errors from the input iterable may propagate.

## Constraints:
    Preconditions:
    - The input iterable must be iterable (support iteration protocol)
    - If the iterable has `__aiter__`, it must be a proper async iterable
    - If the iterable doesn't have `__aiter__`, it must be a proper synchronous iterable
    
    Postconditions:
    - The returned value is always an async iterator
    - All items from the input iterable are yielded exactly once

## Side Effects:
    None - This function is pure and doesn't cause any I/O or external state changes.

## Control Flow:
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start auto_aiter] --> B{hasattr(iterable, "__aiter__")}
    B -- True --> C[async for item in iterable]
    B -- False --> D[for item in iterable]
    C --> E[Yield item]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Return async iterator]
```

## Examples:
```python
# Usage with async iterable
async def async_data_source():
    for i in range(3):
        yield i

async def example():
    async_iter = auto_aiter(async_data_source())
    async for item in async_iter:
        print(item)  # Prints 0, 1, 2

# Usage with sync iterable  
sync_list = [1, 2, 3]
async_iter = auto_aiter(sync_list)
async for item in async_iter:
    print(item)  # Prints 1, 2, 3
```

src.jinja2.async_utils.auto_to_list.documentation:
## Summary:
Converts an async or synchronous iterable into a list asynchronously.

## Description:
This function provides a unified interface for converting both async and synchronous iterables into lists. It leverages `auto_aiter` internally to handle the complexity of determining whether the input is an async iterable or a regular iterable, then collects all items into a list.

## Args:
    value (Union[AsyncIterable[V], Iterable[V]]): An async iterable or regular iterable to convert to a list.

## Returns:
    List[V]: A list containing all items from the input iterable.

## Raises:
    None explicitly raised - relies on underlying iterator behavior.

## Constraints:
    Precondition: The input must be either an async iterable (has `__aiter__` method) or a regular iterable.
    Postcondition: The returned value is always a list containing all elements from the input iterable.

## Side Effects:
    None - this function is pure and doesn't cause any I/O or external state mutations.

## Control Flow:
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start auto_to_list] --> B{Input is AsyncIterable?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use auto_aiter with async for]
    B -- No --> D[Use auto_aiter with regular for]
    C --> E[Collect items into list]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Return list]
```

## Examples:
```python
# With async iterable
async def async_generator():
    for i in range(3):
        yield i

result = await auto_to_list(async_generator())  # Returns [0, 1, 2]

# With regular iterable
result = await auto_to_list([1, 2, 3])  # Returns [1, 2, 3]
```

src.jinja2.debug.get_template_locals.documentation:
## Summary:
Extracts and processes template local variables from a mapping of runtime locals, handling context data and variable overrides based on depth.

## Description:
This function is used in Jinja2 debugging contexts to extract template-local variables from the runtime environment. It combines context data with local variable overrides, resolving conflicts based on variable depth levels. The function is typically called during template rendering or debugging operations to provide access to the effective template variable scope.

## Args:
    real_locals (Mapping[str, Any]): A mapping of local variables from the runtime environment, typically containing template context and local variables prefixed with "l_".

## Returns:
    Dict[str, Any]: A dictionary containing the merged template local variables, including context data and resolved local variable overrides.

## Raises:
    ValueError: When processing local variables with "l_" prefix, if the variable name cannot be split into expected components.

## Constraints:
    Preconditions:
    - Input must be a mapping-like object that supports .get() and .items() methods
    - Local variables with names starting with "l_" are processed for depth-based override resolution
    
    Postconditions:
    - Returned dictionary contains all context data plus resolved local variables
    - Variables with "l_" prefix are properly resolved based on depth ordering
    - Missing variables are properly removed from the result

## Side Effects:
    None - This function is pure and doesn't modify any external state.

## Control Flow:
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start get_template_locals] --> B{ctx exists in real_locals?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Get ctx.get_all().copy()]
    B -- No --> D[data = {}]
    C --> E[data assigned]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Initialize local_overrides dict]
    F --> G[Iterate real_locals items]
    G --> H{name starts with "l_" AND value is not missing?}
    H -- No --> I[Continue to next item]
    H -- Yes --> J[Try split "l_" name]
    J --> K{ValueError from split?}
    K -- Yes --> I
    K -- No --> L[Parse depth from name]
    L --> M[Get current depth for name]
    M --> N{cur_depth < depth?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Update local_overrides[name]]
    N -- No --> P[Skip update]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Process local_overrides entries]
    Q --> R[For each name,value in local_overrides]
    R --> S{value is missing?}
    S -- Yes --> T[Remove name from data]
    S -- No --> U[Set data[name] = value]
    T --> V[Continue]
    U --> V
    V --> W[Return data]
```

## Examples:
    # Basic usage with context
    locals_dict = {"context": template_context, "l_0_var1": "value1", "l_1_var1": "value2"}
    result = get_template_locals(locals_dict)
    # Returns merged context with var1 resolved to "value2" due to higher depth
    
    # Usage without context
    locals_dict = {"l_0_var1": "value1", "l_0_var2": missing}
    result = get_template_locals(locals_dict)
    # Returns {"var1": "value1"} with var2 removed due to missing value

src.jinja2.debug.rewrite_traceback_stack.documentation:
## Summary:
Rewrites traceback information to provide clearer debugging context for Jinja2 template errors by filtering internal frames and mapping template line numbers to source locations.

## Description:
This function processes exception tracebacks to improve debugging experience when Jinja2 templates fail. It removes internal implementation frames from the traceback, maps template line numbers to actual source line numbers, and constructs a cleaner traceback that points to user-facing template code rather than Jinja2's internal machinery.

The function is typically called when handling exceptions during template rendering to provide more meaningful error messages to developers. It's extracted into its own function to encapsulate the complex traceback manipulation logic and maintain clean separation between template execution and error reporting.

## Args:
    source (str, optional): The source code of the template being processed. Defaults to None.

## Returns:
    BaseException: The exception with its traceback rewritten to provide better debugging information.

## Raises:
    None explicitly raised - delegates to underlying exception handling mechanisms.

## Constraints:
    Preconditions:
    - Must be called within an exception handler (sys.exc_info() must contain an active exception)
    - The exception must be either a TemplateSyntaxError or a regular exception
    
    Postconditions:
    - The returned exception has a cleaned-up traceback chain
    - TemplateSyntaxError instances are marked as translated
    - Internal Jinja2 frames are filtered out from the traceback

## Side Effects:
    None - This function only manipulates traceback objects and doesn't perform I/O or modify external state.

## Control Flow:
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Get current exception info] --> B{Is TemplateSyntaxError AND not translated?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Mark as translated]
    C --> D[Set source]
    D --> E[Create fake traceback]
    E --> F[Set tb = fake_traceback result]
    B -- No --> F[Set tb = tb.tb_next]
    F --> G[Initialize empty stack]
    G --> H{tb is not None?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Check if frame is internal code]
    I -- Yes --> J[Skip frame, continue loop]
    J --> K{Template found in globals?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Get corresponding line number]
    L --> M[Create fake traceback for template]
    M --> N[Add to stack]
    K -- No --> O[Add original tb to stack]
    O --> P[Move to next frame]
    P --> H
    H -- No --> Q[Reverse stack order]
    Q --> R[Rebuild traceback links]
    R --> S[Return exception with new traceback]
```

## Examples:
```python
try:
    # Template rendering code that might raise an exception
    template.render(context)
except Exception as e:
    # Rewrite traceback for better debugging
    raise rewrite_traceback_stack(template_source) from e
```

src.jinja2.lexer.Failure.documentation:
## Summary:
A callable error factory that creates and raises template syntax errors with line number and filename context.

## Description:
The Failure class serves as a factory for creating template syntax errors during Jinja2 template lexing and parsing. It encapsulates an error message and error class, allowing for consistent error creation with proper line number and filename information. This class is typically used as a callback or sentinel value in lexer tokenization to indicate parsing failures.

## State:
- message (str): The error message to be included in the raised exception
- error_class (Type[TemplateSyntaxError]): The exception class to raise, defaults to TemplateSyntaxError

## Lifecycle:
- Creation: Instantiate with an error message and optional error class
- Usage: Call the instance with line number and filename arguments to raise the error
- Destruction: No explicit cleanup required as it's a simple factory class

## Method Map:
```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Failure.__init__] --> B[Failure.__call__]
    B --> C[Raise TemplateSyntaxError]
```

## Raises:
- TemplateSyntaxError (or subclass): Raised when the instance is called with lineno and filename arguments

## Example:
```python
# Create an error factory
error_factory = Failure("Unexpected end of template", TemplateSyntaxError)

# Later during lexing, raise the error
error_factory(15, "template.html")
# This raises: TemplateSyntaxError("Unexpected end of template", 15, "template.html")
```

src.jinja2.lexer.OptionalLStrip.documentation:
## Summary:
A tuple subclass used as a marker type for optional left-strip configuration in Jinja2 template parsing.

## Description:
The OptionalLStrip class is a minimal tuple subclass designed to serve as an immutable marker object in Jinja2's template lexer. It inherits from tuple and provides a lightweight mechanism for storing configuration data or markers related to optional left-strip behavior during template parsing.

This class is typically used internally by the Jinja2 lexer to represent optional left-strip configurations or as a marker in parsing state machines where left-whitespace stripping behavior may be conditional.

## State:
- Inherits all state from tuple parent class
- Stores member elements passed to constructor as tuple contents
- No additional instance variables due to `__slots__ = ()`
- Members are arbitrary objects that represent configuration or marker data

## Lifecycle:
- Creation: Instantiate using `OptionalLStrip(*members)` where members are the data to be stored
- Usage: Used as immutable marker objects in lexer processing logic
- Destruction: Managed automatically by Python's garbage collection

## Method Map:
```mermaid
graph TD
    A[OptionalLStrip Constructor] --> B[tuple.__new__()]
    B --> C[Immutable Tuple Storage]
```

## Raises:
- TypeError: If arguments to tuple construction are invalid
- Any exceptions raised by the parent tuple constructor

## Example:
```python
# Create an OptionalLStrip marker
marker = OptionalLStrip('lstrip', True, 2)

# Access as tuple
print(marker[0])  # 'lstrip'
print(len(marker))  # 3

# The object behaves like a tuple
for item in marker:
    print(item)
```

src.jinja2.lexer._Rule.documentation:
## Summary:
Represents a tokenization rule used by Jinja2's lexer to identify and categorize patterns in template syntax.

## Description:
The `_Rule` class defines a single rule for tokenizing Jinja2 template content. It encapsulates a regular expression pattern, the token(s) to emit when the pattern matches, and an optional command for processing the matched content. This class serves as a building block for the lexer's tokenization process, allowing the parser to break down template text into meaningful tokens.

## State:
- `pattern`: t.Pattern[str] - A compiled regular expression pattern used to match template content
- `tokens`: t.Union[str, t.Tuple[str, ...], t.Tuple[Failure]] - Specifies the token(s) to emit when pattern matches. Can be a single token name, multiple token names, or a tuple containing Failure objects for error handling
- `command`: t.Optional[str] - An optional command string that specifies how to process the matched content, or None if no special processing is required

## Lifecycle:
- Creation: Instantiated by providing a compiled regex pattern, token specification, and optional command string
- Usage: Typically accessed as elements in a lexer's rule set during tokenization operations
- Destruction: Managed automatically by Python's garbage collection; no explicit cleanup required

## Method Map:
```mermaid
graph TD
    A[_Rule instantiation] --> B[Lexer uses rule for tokenization]
    B --> C[Pattern matching occurs]
    C --> D[Token(s) emitted based on tokens field]
    D --> E[Optional command execution]
```

## Raises:
- No explicit exceptions raised during initialization since it's a simple NamedTuple
- Exceptions may occur during pattern matching or token emission in the lexer context

## Example:
```python
# Creating a rule for matching whitespace
rule = _Rule(
    pattern=re.compile(r'\s+'),
    tokens='WHITESPACE',
    command=None
)

# Creating a rule for matching variable expressions
rule = _Rule(
    pattern=re.compile(r'\{\{([^}]+)\}\}'),
    tokens=('NAME', 'LPAREN', 'RPAREN'),  # Multiple tokens
    command='parse_variable'
)
```

src.jinja2.lexer.compile_rules.documentation:
## Summary:
Compiles regular expression patterns for token recognition in Jinja2 templates based on environment configuration.

## Description:
Constructs a list of token rules that define how to recognize different template constructs such as comments, blocks, variables, line statements, and line comments. This function extracts the tokenization logic from inline code to enable reusable configuration-based pattern compilation.

## Args:
    environment (Environment): Configuration object containing template syntax delimiters and prefixes for various template constructs.

## Returns:
    list[tuple[str, str]]: A list of tuples where each tuple contains a token type identifier and its corresponding regex pattern string, sorted by pattern length in descending order.

## Raises:
    None explicitly raised in this function.

## Constraints:
    Preconditions:
    - The environment object must have properly initialized string properties: comment_start_string, block_start_string, variable_start_string
    - line_statement_prefix and line_comment_prefix may be None but must be valid strings when present
    
    Postconditions:
    - Returns a list of tuples with consistent structure (token_type, pattern)
    - Rules are sorted by pattern length in descending order for proper matching precedence

## Side Effects:
    None.

## Control Flow:
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start compile_rules] --> B{environment.line_statement_prefix}
    B -- Not None --> C[Add line statement rule]
    B -- None --> D[Skip line statement rule]
    D --> E{environment.line_comment_prefix}
    E -- Not None --> F[Add line comment rule]
    E -- None --> G[Continue]
    G --> H[Sort rules by length desc]
    I[Return processed rules]
    C --> I
    F --> I
```

## Examples:
```python
# Basic usage with default environment
env = Environment()
rules = compile_rules(env)
# Returns list of tuples like [(TOKEN_COMMENT_BEGIN, r'\{#'), (TOKEN_BLOCK_BEGIN, r'\{%), ...]

# With custom delimiters
env = Environment(
    comment_start_string='<!--',
    block_start_string='{%',
    variable_start_string='{{'
)
rules = compile_rules(env)
# Returns rules for custom delimiters
```

src.jinja2.lexer.Lexer.documentation:
## Summary:
A Jinja2 template lexer that converts template source text into structured tokens for parsing.

## Description:
The Lexer class is responsible for tokenizing Jinja2 template source code into meaningful tokens that can be processed by the parser. It handles various template constructs including variables, control blocks, comments, raw sections, and line statements. The lexer maintains state during tokenization and properly handles nested structures like parentheses, brackets, and braces.

This class is typically instantiated by the Jinja2 environment when processing templates, and serves as the entry point for converting template text into a token stream that can be parsed into an abstract syntax tree. It uses regular expressions to match template syntax elements and manages lexer states through a stack-based approach.

## State:
- environment: Environment instance containing template configuration like delimiters and settings
- lstrip_blocks: Boolean indicating whether to strip leading whitespace from blocks
- newline_sequence: String representing the newline sequence to normalize to (typically '\n')
- keep_trailing_newline: Boolean indicating whether to preserve trailing newlines
- rules: Dictionary mapping lexer states to lists of _Rule objects defining token matching patterns
- All attributes are initialized in __init__ and are read-only after construction

## Lifecycle:
- Creation: Instantiate with an Environment object to configure tokenization behavior
- Usage: Call tokenize() method with template source text to get a TokenStream
- Destruction: No explicit cleanup required; uses standard Python garbage collection

## Method Map:
```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Lexer.tokenize] --> B[Lexer.tokeniter]
    B --> C[Lexer.wrap]
    C --> D[TokenStream]
    A --> D
```

## Raises:
- TemplateSyntaxError: When encountering invalid syntax during tokenization (e.g., unmatched brackets, invalid identifiers)
- RuntimeError: When regex patterns yield empty matches or dynamic state resolution fails

## Example:
```python
from jinja2 import Environment
from jinja2.lexer import Lexer

# Create environment with default settings
env = Environment()
lexer = Lexer(env)

# Tokenize a simple template
source = "{{ name }}{% if true %}Hello{% endif %}"
token_stream = lexer.tokenize(source)
for token in token_stream:
    print(f"Line {token.lineno}: {token.type} = {repr(token.value)}")

# Output might include:
# Line 1: TOKEN_VARIABLE_BEGIN = '{{'
# Line 1: TOKEN_NAME = 'name'
# Line 1: TOKEN_VARIABLE_END = '}}'
# Line 1: TOKEN_BLOCK_BEGIN = '{%'
# Line 1: TOKEN_NAME = 'if'
# Line 1: TOKEN_NAME = 'true'
# Line 1: TOKEN_BLOCK_END = '%}'
# Line 1: TOKEN_DATA = 'Hello'
# Line 1: TOKEN_BLOCK_BEGIN = '{%'
# Line 1: TOKEN_NAME = 'endif'
# Line 1: TOKEN_BLOCK_END = '%}'
```
claims: A Jinja2 template lexer that converts template source text into structured tokens for parsing.
The Lexer class is responsible for tokenizing Jinja2 template source code into meaningful tokens that can be processed by the parser.
It handles various template constructs including variables, control blocks, comments, raw sections, and line statements.
The lexer maintains state during tokenization and properly handles nested structures like parentheses, brackets, and braces.

This class is typically instantiated by the Jinja2 environment when processing templates, and serves as the entry point for converting template text into a token stream that can be parsed into an abstract syntax tree.
It uses regular expressions to match template syntax elements and manages lexer states through a stack-based approach.
environment: Environment instance containing template configuration like delimiters and settings
lstrip_blocks: Boolean indicating whether to strip leading whitespace from blocks
newline_sequence: String representing the newline sequence to normalize to (typically '\n')
keep_trailing_newline: Boolean indicating whether to preserve trailing newlines
rules: Dictionary mapping lexer states to lists of _Rule objects defining token matching patterns
All attributes are initialized in __init__ and are read-only after construction
Creation: Instantiate with an Environment object to configure tokenization behavior
Usage: Call tokenize() method with template source text to get a TokenStream
Destruction: No explicit cleanup required; uses standard Python garbage collection
TemplateSyntaxError: When encountering invalid syntax during tokenization (e.g., unmatched brackets, invalid identifiers)
RuntimeError: When regex patterns yield empty matches or dynamic state resolution fails

src.jinja2.lexer.Lexer.tokenize.documentation:
## Summary:
Converts a Jinja2 template source string into a stream of tokens for parsing.

## Description:
The tokenize method processes a Jinja2 template source string and converts it into a TokenStream containing structured tokens that represent the template's syntax elements. This method serves as the entry point for the lexical analysis phase of template processing, breaking down the template into meaningful components that can be parsed by the Jinja2 parser.

This method is separated from the core tokenization logic to provide a clean interface that handles the orchestration between the token iteration process and the token wrapping process, ensuring proper construction of the final TokenStream object.

## Args:
    source (str): The Jinja2 template source code to tokenize
    name (Optional[str]): Name of the template for error reporting purposes
    filename (Optional[str]): Filename of the template for error reporting purposes  
    state (Optional[str]): Initial parsing state ('variable', 'block', or None for root)

## Returns:
    TokenStream: An iterator over tokens representing the parsed template structure

## Raises:
    TemplateSyntaxError: When encountering invalid syntax in the template source

## State Changes:
    Attributes READ: None - this method doesn't read any instance attributes directly
    Attributes WRITTEN: None - this method doesn't modify any instance attributes

## Constraints:
    Preconditions: 
    - source must be a valid string
    - state, if provided, must be either 'variable', 'block', or None
    
    Postconditions:
    - Returns a TokenStream object with properly formatted tokens
    - All tokens in the stream are valid Jinja2 syntax elements

## Side Effects:
    None - this method is pure and doesn't cause any external I/O or state changes beyond returning a TokenStream

src.jinja2.lexer.Lexer.tokeniter.documentation:
## Summary:
Generates an iterator of tokens from Jinja2 template source code by applying regex patterns according to lexer rules and maintaining parsing state.

## Description:
This method performs lexical analysis on Jinja2 template source code, breaking it down into structured tokens that represent the various elements of the template syntax. It uses a state machine approach with a stack-based parser to handle nested constructs like blocks, variables, and comments.

The method processes source text through a series of regex patterns defined in `self.rules` based on the current parsing state. It handles complex parsing scenarios including:
- Newline normalization and handling based on `self.keep_trailing_newline`
- Block and variable parsing contexts with proper state transitions
- Operator matching and balancing with `balancing_stack` 
- Whitespace stripping logic controlled by `self.lstrip_blocks`
- Dynamic state transitions based on regex group matches
- Error handling for unmatched characters and unbalanced operators

This method is typically called by `Lexer.tokenize()` to convert template source into a token stream.

## Args:
    source (str): The Jinja2 template source code to tokenize
    name (Optional[str]): Name of the template for error reporting
    filename (Optional[str]): Filename for error reporting  
    state (Optional[str]): Initial parsing state ('variable' or 'block'), defaults to None

## Returns:
    Iterator[Tuple[int, str, str]]: An iterator yielding tuples of (line_number, token_type, token_value)

## Raises:
    TemplateSyntaxError: When encountering unexpected characters or unbalanced operators
    RuntimeError: When regex patterns yield empty strings without stack changes or dynamic resolution fails

## State Changes:
    Attributes READ: 
    - self.rules: Dictionary mapping parsing states to rule lists
    - self.keep_trailing_newline: Controls trailing newline handling
    - self.lstrip_blocks: Controls automatic whitespace stripping
    
    Attributes WRITTEN: 
    - None (method is read-only with respect to instance state)

## Constraints:
    Preconditions:
    - source must be a valid string
    - state, if provided, must be either None, "root", "variable", or "block"
    - All required constants (TOKEN_*) must be defined in the module scope
    - self.rules must contain appropriate rule sets for all expected parsing states
    
    Postconditions:
    - Method returns an iterator that yields properly formatted token tuples
    - Parsing state is maintained correctly through the stack mechanism
    - All opening operators have matching closing operators

## Side Effects:
    None (pure function with respect to external state)

src.jinja2.lexer._describe_token_type.documentation:
## Summary:
Translates internal token type identifiers into human-readable descriptive strings.

## Description:
This utility function converts internal token type constants into descriptive strings that aid in debugging and error reporting. It provides meaningful labels for different types of tokens encountered during template parsing.

The function implements a two-tier lookup mechanism:
1. First checks if the token type exists in a reverse operators mapping
2. Falls back to a predefined dictionary of standard template token descriptions
3. Returns the original token type if no description is found

This separation of concerns allows for clean abstraction of token identification logic and improves maintainability.

## Args:
    token_type (str): An internal identifier representing a token type in the Jinja2 template lexer

## Returns:
    str: Human-readable description of the token type, or the original token_type if no description is available

## Raises:
    None explicitly raised

## Constraints:
    Preconditions:
    - The token_type parameter must be a string
    - The function relies on external constants and mappings (reverse_operators, various TOKEN_* constants) being properly defined in the module scope
    
    Postconditions:
    - Always returns a string value
    - If token_type matches a key in reverse_operators, returns the mapped value from that dictionary
    - If token_type matches keys in the internal description dictionary, returns the corresponding description
    - If no match is found in either lookup mechanism, returns the original token_type unchanged

## Side Effects:
    None

## Control Flow:
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start _describe_token_type] --> B{token_type in reverse_operators?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Return reverse_operators[token_type]]
    B -- No --> D[Lookup in token description dict]
    D --> E{Match found?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Return description]
    E -- No --> G[Return token_type]
```

## Examples:
    # Example usage in error reporting
    token_desc = _describe_token_type("TOKEN_COMMENT_BEGIN")
    # Returns: "begin of comment"
    
    # Example with operator token
    op_desc = _describe_token_type("LT")
    # Returns: the mapped operator description from reverse_operators (if LT exists as a key)
    
    # Example with unknown token
    unknown_desc = _describe_token_type("UNKNOWN_TOKEN")
    # Returns: "UNKNOWN_TOKEN" (original token_type)

src.jinja2.lexer.Token.documentation:
## Summary:
Represents a lexical token produced by Jinja2's template lexer, containing line number, token type, and token value.

## Description:
The Token class serves as a fundamental data structure in Jinja2's template processing pipeline. It encapsulates the basic information about a parsed element from a template, including its position (line number), classification (type), and content (value). Tokens are created by the lexer during template parsing and are subsequently processed by the parser to construct the abstract syntax tree.

This class provides utility methods for pattern matching against token types and values, making it easier for the parser to recognize and handle different token sequences. The test() method supports both simple type matching and type-value pair matching using colon-separated patterns.

## State:
- lineno: int - The line number in the source template where this token was found. Must be a positive integer.
- type: str - The classification of the token (e.g., 'name', 'number', 'operator'). Must be a string identifier.
- value: str - The actual text content of the token. For example, for a NAME token, this would be the variable name.

## Lifecycle:
- Creation: Tokens are instantiated by the Jinja2 lexer during template parsing. They are typically created with three arguments: line number, token type string, and token value string.
- Usage: Once created, tokens are immutable and are primarily used for pattern matching via the test() and test_any() methods. The __str__() method provides human-readable representations for debugging and error reporting.
- Destruction: No explicit cleanup is required as tokens are simple data structures managed by Python's garbage collector.

## Method Map:
```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Token Creation] --> B[Token.__str__()]
    A --> C[Token.test()]
    A --> D[Token.test_any()]
    C --> E[Pattern Matching]
    D --> E
    B --> F[Debug Output]
```

## Raises:
- None explicitly raised by Token.__init__ since it inherits from NamedTuple
- The test() method may raise AttributeError if expr is not a string (though this is unlikely due to type hints)

## Example:
```python
# Creating a token
token = Token(5, 'NAME', 'username')

# Using string representation
print(str(token))  # Uses describe_token() for readable output

# Pattern matching - simple type matching
if token.test('NAME'):
    print("Found a variable name")

# Pattern matching - type-value pair matching
if token.test('NAME:username'):
    print("Found the username variable")

# Multiple pattern matching
if token.test_any('NAME', 'NUMBER', 'STRING'):
    print("Found a simple value")
```

src.jinja2.lexer.describe_token.documentation:
## Summary
Returns a human-readable string representation of a Jinja2 template token, with special handling for name tokens.

## Description
This function provides a textual description of a Jinja2 template token for debugging and error reporting purposes. When the token represents a name (variable, keyword, etc.), it returns the token's raw value. For all other token types, it uses a lookup table to provide descriptive names.

The function is primarily used internally by the Token class's `__str__` method to enable easy printing and debugging of tokens.

## Args
    token (Token): A Jinja2 token object containing line number, type, and value information

## Returns
    str: For TOKEN_NAME tokens, returns the token's value directly. For other token types, returns a descriptive string representing the token type.

## Raises
    None explicitly raised

## Constraints
    Preconditions:
        - The token parameter must be a valid Token instance with properly initialized lineno, type, and value fields
        - The token.type field must be a string that can be processed by _describe_token_type for non-TOKEN_NAME cases
    
    Postconditions:
        - Always returns a string representation of the token
        - For TOKEN_NAME tokens, the returned string matches the token's value exactly

## Side Effects
    None

## Control Flow
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[describe_token called with token] --> B{token.type == TOKEN_NAME?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Return token.value]
    B -->|No| D[Call _describe_token_type(token.type)]
    C --> E[Return]
    D --> E
```

## Examples
    # For a TOKEN_NAME token representing a variable
    token = Token(1, TOKEN_NAME, "username")
    describe_token(token)  # Returns "username"
    
    # For a BLOCK_BEGIN token
    token = Token(1, TOKEN_BLOCK_BEGIN, "{%")
    describe_token(token)  # Returns "begin of statement block"
```

## Summary:
Returns a human-readable string representation of the token, showing either its value for name tokens or a descriptive label for other token types.

## Description:
This method provides a string representation of the token that helps in debugging and logging token streams. It delegates to the `describe_token` function which determines the appropriate representation based on the token type. For tokens of type `TOKEN_NAME`, it returns the token's value directly. For all other token types, it returns a descriptive label based on the token type.

## Args:

    Attributes WRITTEN: None

## Constraints:
    Preconditions: The token object must be properly initialized with lineno, type, and value attributes.
    Postconditions: The returned string is a valid representation of the token's identity and purpose in the template parsing process.

## Side Effects:
    None

src.jinja2.lexer.Token.test.documentation:
## Summary:
Tests whether the token matches a given expression pattern, supporting both simple type matching and type-value pair matching.

## Description:
This method determines if the current token matches a specified expression pattern. It supports two matching modes: simple type matching (when the expression equals the token's type) and type-value pair matching (when the expression follows the format "type:value" and matches both the token's type and value).

## Args:
    expr (str): The expression to match against. Can be either:
        - A simple string that matches the token's type
        - A colon-separated string in the format "type:value" that matches both the token's type and value

## Returns:
    bool: True if the token matches the expression pattern, False otherwise.

## Raises:
    None: This method does not raise any exceptions.

## State Changes:
    Attributes READ: self.type, self.value
    Attributes WRITTEN: None

## Constraints:
    Preconditions: The token must have valid type and value attributes (both strings)
    Postconditions: The method returns a boolean indicating match status without modifying the token

## Side Effects:
    None: This method performs no I/O operations or external service calls and does not mutate any objects outside the token itself.

src.jinja2.lexer.TokenStream.documentation:
## Summary:
A stream-based iterator for managing sequences of tokens in Jinja2 template parsing, supporting lookahead, token pushing, and expectation validation.

## Description:
The TokenStream class provides a buffered iterator interface over a sequence of Token objects, commonly used during Jinja2 template compilation. It enables parsers to consume tokens sequentially while providing mechanisms to peek ahead, push tokens back, and validate expected token types. The class maintains an internal buffer for pushed tokens and handles EOF transitions gracefully.

This abstraction is essential for implementing recursive descent parsers that need to look ahead at upcoming tokens without permanently consuming them, making it a core component in Jinja2's template parsing pipeline.

## State:
- `_iter`: Iterator over the underlying token generator, initialized from constructor argument
- `_pushed`: Deque containing tokens that have been pushed back onto the stream
- `name`: Optional string identifier for the template being parsed
- `filename`: Optional string identifying the source file of the template
- `closed`: Boolean flag indicating whether the stream has been closed
- `current`: Current Token object representing the next token to be consumed

## Lifecycle:
- Creation: Instantiate with a token generator (producing Token objects), optional name, and optional filename
- Usage: Iterate using `next()` or `__next__()`, or use convenience methods like `look()`, `skip()`, `expect()` to validate and advance
- Destruction: Automatically closed when exhausted or manually via `close()` method

## Method Map:
```mermaid
graph TD
    A[TokenStream.__init__] --> B[TokenStream.__iter__]
    B --> C[TokenStreamIterator]
    C --> D[TokenStream.__next__]
    D --> E{Has pushed tokens?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Pop from _pushed]
    E -->|No| G[Get from _iter]
    G --> H{StopIteration?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Close stream]
    I --> J[Return current token]
    D --> K[Update current token]
    K --> L[Return previous current token]
    
    M[TokenStream.look] --> N[Next token]
    N --> O[Push result back]
    O --> P[Restore old token]
    P --> Q[Return result]
    
    R[TokenStream.expect] --> S[Check token type]
    S --> T{Match?}
    T -->|No| U[Throw TemplateSyntaxError]
    T -->|Yes| V[Consume token]
    V --> W[Return consumed token]
```

## Raises:
- `TemplateSyntaxError`: Raised by `expect()` method when the current token doesn't match the expected expression, particularly when encountering EOF

## Example:
```python
# Create a token stream from a generator
stream = TokenStream(token_generator, "my_template", "/path/to/template")

# Consume tokens sequentially
token1 = next(stream)
token2 = next(stream)

# Look ahead at next token without consuming it
next_token = stream.look()

# Skip tokens
stream.skip(2)

# Expect a specific token type and advance to next token
expected_token = stream.expect("NAME")

# Close the stream when done
stream.close()
```

It enables parsers to consume tokens sequentially while providing mechanisms to peek ahead, push tokens back, and validate expected token types.
The class maintains an internal buffer for pushed tokens and handles EOF transitions gracefully.

This abstraction is essential for implementing recursive descent parsers that need to look ahead at upcoming tokens without permanently consuming them, making it a core component in Jinja2's template parsing pipeline.

src.jinja2.utils.Cycler.next.documentation:
## Summary:
Returns the current item from the cycler and advances to the next item in the cycle.

## Description:
This method retrieves the item at the current position in the cycler's sequence, increments the internal position counter, and returns the retrieved item. It implements circular iteration through the items, wrapping around to the beginning when the end is reached.

## Args:
    None

## Returns:
    t.Any: The current item from the cycler's sequence before advancing the position.

src.jinja2.lexer.TokenStreamIterator.documentation:
## Summary:
An iterator that traverses a token stream, yielding individual tokens one at a time until reaching the end of file.

## Description:
The TokenStreamIterator provides a Python iterator interface for consuming tokens from a TokenStream. It enables sequential processing of template tokens by implementing the iterator protocol. When the end of file token (TOKEN_EOF) is encountered, the iterator automatically closes the underlying token stream and raises StopIteration to signal completion.

## State:
- stream: TokenStream instance containing the tokens to iterate over
- The stream attribute holds the reference to the token stream being consumed
- No other instance attributes are maintained

## Lifecycle:
- Creation: Instantiated with a TokenStream object
- Usage: Used in for-loops or with next() function calls to consume tokens sequentially
- Destruction: Automatically closes the underlying stream when StopIteration is raised upon encountering TOKEN_EOF

## Method Map:
```mermaid
graph TD
    A[TokenStreamIterator.__init__] --> B[stream set]
    B --> C[TokenStreamIterator.__iter__]
    C --> D[TokenStreamIterator.__next__]
    D --> E{token.type is TOKEN_EOF?}
    E -->|Yes| F[stream.close()]
    F --> G[StopIteration]
    E -->|No| H[next(stream)]
    H --> D
```

## Raises:
- StopIteration: Raised when the end of file token (TOKEN_EOF) is encountered, signaling the end of iteration

## Example:
```python
# Create a token stream (assumed to exist)
token_stream = TokenStream(template_source)

# Create iterator
iterator = TokenStreamIterator(token_stream)

# Iterate through tokens
for token in iterator:
    print(f"Token type: {token.type}, value: {token.value}")

# Or consume manually
iterator = TokenStreamIterator(token_stream)
try:
    while True:
        token = next(iterator)
        print(f"Token: {token}")
except StopIteration:
    print("End of token stream")
```

It enables sequential processing of template tokens by implementing the iterator protocol.
When the end of file token (TOKEN_EOF) is encountered, the iterator automatically closes the underlying token stream and raises StopIteration to signal completion.
